title: Hard-coded credentials are security-sensitive
description: This rule flags instances of hard-coded credentials used in database and LDAP connections. It looks for hard-coded credentials in connection strings, and for variable names that match any of the patterns from the provided list. Credentials allow access to a sensitive component like a database, a file storage, an API or a service.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
string username = "admin";
string password = "Admin123"; // Sensitive
string usernamePassword  = "user=admin&password=Admin123"; // Sensitive
string url = "scheme://user:Admin123@domain.com"; // Sensitive

logic.detection_conditions: Detect assignment statements where the variable name contains credential-related keywords (password, pwd, passwd, credential, secret) AND the assigned value is a hard-coded string literal (not a method call, not string interpolation)
logic.triggering_conditions: (Variable name matches credential patterns) AND (Assigned value is string literal) AND (NOT in excluded contexts)
logic.reasoning: This rule detects hard-coded credentials by identifying assignment statements where credential-related variable names are assigned string literals. The regex pattern matches common credential variable names (password, pwd, passwd, credential, secret, apipassword, dbpassword) followed by assignment to a string literal. Comprehensive exclusion patterns prevent false positives on: configuration retrieval methods, string formatting/interpolation, logging statements, property/constant names, known test credentials, and comments. This AST-first approach targets Assignment nodes specifically.
